perturbation.original_text: Cargill will perform the Work Plan in a workmanlike manner with reasonable skill and care ordinarily exercised by members of the profession practicing under similar conditions and in accordance with accepted industry practices and professional guidelines;
perturbation.changed_text: Cargill will attempt to perform the Work Plan in a manner it deems appropriate, considering the circumstances.
perturbation.explanation: This change replaces the objective standard of 'workmanlike manner with reasonable skill and care' with a subjective standard of 'in a manner it deems appropriate, considering the circumstances.' This weakens the obligation and makes it difficult to enforce.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statutes § 326B.081 Subd. 11 requires that construction work be performed in a 'good and workmanlike manner'. The modified text replaces this objective standard with a subjective one, contradicting the requirement for a specific standard of workmanship.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 326B.081 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 326B Section 326B.081 326B.075 326B.082 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 326B Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 326B.081 Version List Recent History 2024 Subd. 3 Amended 2024 c 127 art 10 s 13 2024 Subd. 6 Amended 2024 c 127 art 10 s 14 2024 Subd. 8 Amended 2024 c 127 art 10 s 15 2013 Subd. 3 Amended 2013 c 85 art 2 s 4 2012 Subd. 3 Amended 2012 c 295 art 2 s 12 2012 Subd. 3 Amended 2012 c 248 s 2 2007 326B.081 New 2007 c 140 art 3 s 1 326B.081 DEFINITIONS. § Subdivision 1. Application. For purposes of sections 326B.081 to 326B.085 , the terms defined in this section have the meanings given them. § Subd. 2. Administrative order. "Administrative order" means an order issued under section 326B.082, subdivision 7 . § Subd. 3. Applicable law. "Applicable law" means the provisions of sections 181.165 , 181.722 , 181.723 , 325E.66 , 327.31 to 327.36 , this chapter, and chapter 341, and all rules, orders, stipulation agreements, settlements, compliance agreements, licenses, registrations, certificates, and permits adopted, issued, or enforced by the department under sections 181.165 , 181.722 , 181.723, 325E.66, 327.31 to 327.36 , this chapter, or chapter 341. § Subd. 4. Document or documents. "Document" or "documents" includes papers; books; records; memoranda; data; contracts; drawings; graphs; charts; photographs; digital, video, and audio recordings; records; accounts; files; statements; letters; emails; invoices; bills; notes; and calendars maintained in any form or manner. § Subd. 5. Final. "Final" when used to d
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces the original objective standard of workmanship with a subjective standard that Cargill deems appropriate. According to the law explanation, Minnesota Statutes § 326B.081 Subd. 11 requires construction work to be performed in a 'good and workmanlike manner'. The Perturbation explanation states this weakens the obligation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 shows Sec. 326B.081 MN Statutes, confirming the existence of this statute. The change directly contradicts the standard required, as the changed text allows for a standard lower than 'good and workmanlike'. The ambiguity introduced creates a contradiction, as the contractual obligation is changed to a subjective standard, rather than being bound to the objective standard required by law.

perturbation.original_text: Subject to Section 13.1, Cargill has in effect and will maintain in effect all permits, licenses and other authorizations necessary to perform the Work Plan;
perturbation.changed_text: Subject to Section 13.1, Cargill believes it has and will try to maintain permits, licenses and other authorizations it thinks are needed to perform the Work Plan;.
perturbation.explanation: The phrases 'believes it has', 'try to maintain', and 'thinks are needed' introduce ambiguity. Instead of a guarantee of possessing and maintaining necessary permits, licenses, and authorizations, it becomes a statement of belief and intent to try, making enforcement difficult. This is problematic if Cargill does not actually have the correct licenses to perform the development, thereby violating Minnesota regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statute § 16A.695 prohibits state contracts with entities that are not in compliance with state law. Modifying the section to suggest Cargill may not have all licenses and permits would create conflict with that regulation if Cargill bids on any Minnesota state business, as the state requires full compliance with all applicable state laws.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 16A.695 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes ADMINISTRATION AND FINANCE Chapter 16A Section 16A.695 16A.69 16A.70 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 16A Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 16A.695 Version List Recent History 2007 Subd. 2 Amended 2007 c 148 art 2 s 14 2007 Subd. 3 Amended 2007 c 148 art 2 s 15 2007 Subd. 6 New 2007 c 148 art 2 s 16 2007 Subd. 7 New 2007 c 148 art 2 s 17 2007 Subd. 8 New 2007 c 148 art 2 s 18 2007 Subd. 9 New 2007 c 148 art 2 s 19 2004 Subd. 3 Amended 2004 c 278 s 1 1996 Subd. 5 New 1996 c 463 s 32 1995 Subd. 1 Amended 1995 c 2 art 1 s 19 1995 Subd. 2 Amended 1995 c 2 art 1 s 20 1995 Subd. 3 Amended 1995 c 2 art 1 s 21 1995 Subd. 3a New 1995 c 2 art 1 s 22 1994 16A.695 New 1994 c 643 s 36 16A.695 STATE BOND FINANCED PROPERTY. § Subdivision 1. Definitions. (a) The definitions in this subdivision apply to this section. (b) "State bond financed property" means property acquired or bettered in whole or in part with the proceeds of state general obligation bonds authorized to be issued under article XI, section 5, clause (a), of the Minnesota Constitution. (c) "Public officer or agency" means a state officer or agency, the University of Minnesota, the Minnesota Historical Society, and any county, home rule charter or statutory city, school district, special purpose district, or other public entity, or any officer or employee thereof. (d) "Fair market value" means, with respect to the sale of state bond financed property, the price that would be paid by a willing and qualified buyer to a willing and qualified seller as determined by an appraisal of the property, or the price bid b
perturbation.justification: The original text states Cargill 'has in effect and will maintain' permits, a clear obligation. The changed text introduces ambiguity with phrases like 'believes it has', 'try to maintain', and 'thinks are needed'. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that this weakens the guarantee of possessing necessary permits, making enforcement difficult and potentially violating Minnesota regulations if Cargill lacks the correct licenses. The Law Explanation points to Minnesota Statute § 16A.695, which prohibits state contracts with entities not in compliance with state law. Although the scraped snippet primarily defines 'State bond financed property,' the Law Explanation's assertion that the statute requires full compliance with all applicable state laws, combined with the weakening of Cargill's obligation, suggests a potential contradiction. The modified text creates an ambiguous situation where Cargill's compliance is uncertain, which could conflict with the state's requirement for full compliance.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees to take and maintain proper and appropriate steps to protect Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party will consider taking some steps it feels are adequate at the time to protect Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: This change weakens the obligation to protect Confidential Information. Instead of agreeing to 'take and maintain proper and appropriate steps,' the Receiving Party only agrees to 'consider taking some steps it feels are adequate at the time.' This introduces ambiguity and subjectivity, making it difficult to enforce any specific standard of confidentiality. This contradicts the Minnesota Trade Secrets Act, which requires reasonable efforts to maintain confidentiality.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Minnesota Trade Secrets Act requires 'reasonable efforts' to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. The modified text allows the Receiving Party to take only steps they deem adequate, which may fall short of the 'reasonable efforts' required by law, hence contradicting it.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325C.01 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325C Section 325C.01 325C.02 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325C Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325C.01 Version List 325C.01 DEFINITIONS. § Subdivision 1. Scope. As used in sections 325C.01 to 325C.07 , the terms defined in these sections have the meanings given them, unless the context requires otherwise. § Subd. 2. Improper means. "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. § Subd. 3. Misappropriation. "Misappropriation" means: (i) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the discloser's or user's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) before a material change of the discloser's or user's position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. § Subd. 4. Person.
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect Confidential Information, introducing ambiguity. The original text states the Receiving Party 'agrees to take and maintain proper and appropriate steps,' while the changed text says the party will only 'consider taking some steps it feels are adequate.' This contradicts the principle of 'reasonable efforts' required to maintain secrecy, which the law explanation associates with the Minnesota Trade Secrets Act. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not explicitly mention 'reasonable efforts,' it defines 'misappropriation,' which includes the 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent' and situations where there is 'a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use.' The change weakens the obligation to maintain secrecy, making it more difficult to prove misappropriation.
